method2.generated_article:
==History==
Prehistoric activity around Jethou is indicated by late Palaeolithic flint scatters on the seabed between Crevichon and Jethou, which are exposed at very low spring tides.[5] The island also appears in studies of prehistoric movement in the Channel Islands, where Guernsey, Herm, Jethou and Sark would have been visible from nearby coasts and may have attracted early seafarers.[4] Jethou’s wider archaeological record has been described as extending back to prehistoric times, including evidence such as menhirs.[1]

Historically, Jethou formed one of the smaller islands associated with Guernsey in the Channel Islands, an archipelago that has been politically linked with Britain since 1204 while retaining strong Norman and francophone cultural traditions until relatively recent times.[3] Its name and early history have been associated in modern accounts with Viking origins, and later coverage has described its development as a private island.[1] The island’s rocky shores also made it a hazard to shipping, with 19th-century writing describing a vessel that ran onto Jethou in dense fog while returning from a Spanish port.[2]

In modern times Jethou has remained privately occupied rather than publicly accessible, despite being owned by the States of Guernsey.[1] Since 1991 it has been leased to British businessman Sir Peter Ogden, and it is generally closed to the public apart from a caretaker and occasional visitors.[1] Media coverage in the 21st century has continued to emphasize the island’s privacy, isolation and restricted access, including rare public visiting opportunities.[1]

==Governance==
Jethou is within the Bailiwick of Guernsey, a Crown Dependency that is not part of the United Kingdom or Great Britain.[1] The island is administered by the government of Guernsey, while Alderney and Sark are separate jurisdictions within the Bailiwick with their own legislatures.[5] As part of the Crown Dependency system, defence and foreign affairs are traditionally the responsibility of the British Government, and ultimate responsibility for good governance lies with the Crown through the Lord Chancellor.[1] Jethou is a leased island; it was advertised in 1983 on a 24-year lease, and later accounts identify it, along with Herm, as one of the Bailiwick's leased islands.[4][5] Unlike Herm, which is required to remain open to the public between sunrise and sunset, Jethou is currently not open to the public.[4] The island is included in the Herm, Jethou and The Humps Ramsar site, designated in 2014 as the Bailiwick's second Ramsar site under Guernsey's participation in the international wetlands convention.[2] Conservation policy for Jethou is also addressed within Bailiwick environmental reporting, including the State of Nature report for Guernsey, Lihou, Herm, Jethou and The Humps, which is intended to guide conservation action by government, industry, charities, businesses and the public.[2]

==Wildlife==
Jethou forms part of the Herm, Jethou and The Humps Ramsar site, which was designated in 2015 as a wetland of international importance in the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[4] The site is a shallow marine ecosystem around Herm, Jethou and The Humps, with a tidal range of up to 10 metres.[1] Its habitats include reefs, seabeds, shipwrecks, eelgrass beds and maerl gravel.[2] These marine habitats provide spawning and nursery grounds for fish including European sea bass and Atlantic mackerel, and the area also supports species such as black sea bream.[1][2] Notable wildlife recorded from the wider Ramsar site includes Atlantic grey seals, basking sharks, the green ormer, the Balearic shearwater and puffins.[1][2] The islands of Herm, Jethou and The Humps are also included in local State of Nature reporting for Guernsey, which monitors habitats and species to guide conservation efforts.[3]

==Jethou in popular culture==
Jethou has been represented in philately through local stamp issues, similar to those produced for Lihou, with design input from Charles Coker.[1] Its first stamp issue appeared in 1960, shortly after Group Captain William Hedley Cliff and his American wife took over the island’s lease from the Crown in 1958.[1] The set depicted symbols of island identity, including Jethou’s crest, a map of the island, its tidal islets Fauconnière and Crevichon, and an island scene with a Neolithic standing stone.[1]
